course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Matrix Inverses and Determinant Properties
sessionGoal: Students should be able to use inverses and determinant properties without turning every problem into a long row-reduction problem.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by saying: an inverse undoes a matrix, and a determinant tells us if an inverse exists.
Do Problem 1 slowly because it connects inverse matrices to solving systems.
Use Problem 2 to practice determinant shortcuts.
Use Problem 3 to emphasize parameter values that make a matrix singular.
Point students to Practice Lab Sections 4-5 afterward.
sayThisFirst: A matrix has an inverse only when its determinant is nonzero.
If A is invertible, then Ax=b has exactly one solution: x=A^{-1}b.
Do not compute an inverse unless the problem actually asks for it.
Determinant properties are shortcuts. Use them before doing arithmetic.
boardPlan: Write: invertible ⇔ det(A) ≠ 0 ⇔ Ax=b has one solution for every b.
Do Problem 1: inverse plus solving Ax=b.
Do Problem 2: determinant properties only, no row reduction.
Do Problem 3: find when a parameter makes det(A)=0.
keyFormulas: If A is n by n, det(cA)=c^n det(A).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the n in det(cA)=c^n det(A).
Writing det(A+B)=det(A)+det(B), which is not a rule.
Trying to invert a matrix before checking whether the determinant is zero.
Mixing up A^{-1}b with bA^{-1}; vectors go on the correct side.
notationWarnings: Use A^{-1}, not 1/A, for a matrix inverse.
Write det(A), not |A|, if students might confuse it with absolute value.
For a 2 by 2 inverse, keep the sign changes in the off-diagonal entries.

problems.title: Use a 2 by 2 inverse to solve a system
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: first check the determinant. If it is nonzero, the inverse exists and we can use x=A^{-1}b.

drawInstructions: Draw Ax=b as: A sends unknown x to known b.
Draw A^{-1} as the step that sends b back to x.
Emphasize that the inverse exists because det(A)=1, not 0.

walkthrough.label: Check that the inverse exists
walkthrough.latex: \[\det(A)=2(3)-1(5)=6-5=1.\] Since the determinant is nonzero, \(A^{-1}\) exists.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to divide by the determinant. Here the determinant is 1, so it looks invisible.
Swapping the wrong entries in the 2 by 2 inverse formula.
Multiplying b on the wrong side.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 for inverses and transformations.

problems.title: Use determinant properties instead of row reduction
problems.studentPromptLatex: Suppose \(A\) is a \(3\times 3\) matrix with \(\det(A)=-3\). Compute \(\det(2A^T A^{-1})\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this is not asking us to know A. We only need determinant rules.

drawInstructions: Write the expression as a product of three determinant pieces.
Circle the scalar rule det(2B)=2^3 det(B).
Point out that A^T and A have the same determinant.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 for determinant properties.

problems.title: Find parameter values that make a matrix non-invertible
problems.studentPromptLatex: For what values of \(k\) is \[A(k)=\begin{bmatrix}1&2&0\\0&k-1&1\\0&3&k+1\end{bmatrix}\] not invertible?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: not invertible means determinant equals zero. We do not need the inverse.

drawInstructions: Highlight the first column because it makes expansion easy.
Write det(A(k)) as a 2 by 2 determinant after expansion.
Mark the values where the determinant becomes zero.

walkthrough.label: Expand using the first column
walkthrough.latex: The first column has two zeros, so \[\det(A(k))=1\cdot\det\begin{bmatrix}k-1&1\\3&k+1\end{bmatrix}.\]

commonMistakes: Forgetting that not invertible means singular.
Expanding along a hard row or column instead of using the zeros.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 for determinants and invertibility.

wrapUpQuestions: What are three equivalent ways to say a matrix is invertible?
When should you use determinant properties instead of row reducing?
Why does det(cA) depend on the matrix size?

linksToWebsite.note: Practice Lab Section(s) 4, 5 for more practice.

qualityControlNotes: Checked A inverse: det=1 and A^{-1}b=(-1,6).
reviewSummary: Session 10 emphasizes inverses as tools and determinant properties as shortcuts.
